the kids because they were they took advantage of her she was too kind and they would the kids would act up she liked the kids but she didn't like trying to teach them but teaching the army men who there was no discipline problem yeah because her officers were right on the base there you know she even had a sergeant that couldn't read danger minefield these guys all had you know whenever anybody said what does that sign up there say they say oh gee i just got something in my eye you know oh man but when she uh taught them to read and write they were just overjoyed and they thought they were on top of the world that's good anyway during that summer i read direct's book and worked my way through it and i didn't know it but drac is considered not only one of the greatest theorists of our anymore so that was uh my big moment in grad school doing a thesis was fine um i defended it nicely and then i sort of get defended again at an international conference in london and uh it was actually when i when i presented it at the committee of the physical society in washington it got written up in the newspaper there i actually interviewed my police supervisor me but um anyway so that was uh interesting so where did you decide to have i graduated we were married on graduation day by the college pastor on the front lawn of this gorgeous house and lawn and gardens that we lived at i lived in and the chairs were all on the lawn there and my wife's father led her down the aisle between the chairs the rows of chairs were right in front of a big tree and then i appeared from behind helped me repair the car he had a job in dayton also i was in kettering's home up on a high hill overlooking southern dayton ohio in a state he was of course fabulously wealthy he was the guy that he started he was when he was a young man he uh had to leave ohio state and go back to the farm because of an illness he had and probably was there he got well and he got a job been dating this this he told me okay personally he had a job in dayton with a dayton electric company they said we need to motorize cash registers and uh all of our engineers have told us you can't do it because the engine the motor would have to be as big as the cash register to force open the drawer instantly and so on he said that after he looked at it a while he realized that you didn't need much of a motor because when all the motor doesn't push the door toward open and then it's not the motor can cool off while the guy makes a change and pushes the drawer back in before it's used again so you can have a small motor that made his fortune to start out he was then hired by uh to form delco dayton electric company that um general was a division of general motors that did all their electrical develop parts for their cars and then he uh became vice president for research and development but he was despite having this big home he was a simple guy i mean his wife olive had the largest cadillac ever built built for her custom built with olive color but he had a chevy a black chevy and when he come in on the airplane to a board meeting or something like that or meeting of the executives the other these cadillacs would be lined up on the airstrip and his little black chevrolet with his with his uh household caretaker it was also his chauffeur and the caretaker's wife mended his clothes he never bought a new suit he would like to come down and sit in my office which was his let this lab that i was working in for him was the lower part of his house extended out over the edge of this dust sloping downward hill overlooking the miami valley you know a great view from my desk with all glass on the end of my office he'd come down and sit there and talk with me i was the uh the rest of people were all biologists and chemists i was representing physics which i when they'd asked me a question i would quickly figure out read up on it and get back to them uh this was a co-op job so that was great in beating him and seeing his daughter-in-law come in sometimes with gas on the weekend to show them the house and the lab that she and her husband would eventually inherit and we had to clean up the lab and have all the wires straight and flowers around and so on for burger visits anyway that was an interesting in itself and when he got a girlfriend she was about to beside herself but he never married the girlfriend so her son didn't inherit the place anyway the director of the lab was quite an elegant dresser and one day he said that his car was usually being used by his wife or something like that and he needed to ride home could he have a ride home so we wrote him so we i moved over and he got into this home built station wagon a homemade by my roommate that had you know an engine from one kind of company and parts of the body from other companies and all sorts of stuff have been thrown away by other people so anyway as we were going along the the director said thrilling isn't it and when he got out of rip his pants anyway gives you a little flavor of things anyway when i got out i went to graduate school got my phd there did a post doc there and uh was hired by i thought i'd like to teach you a small college i was hired by a small university bucknell university in the mountains of pennsylvania and uh after two years there during that time my um there were only a couple of majors in this small university what was really nice about it was that it was like rochester where i went to graduate school and that they had a program of bringing prominent speakers the librarian had in his treasure room and he was supervised the making of the coffee by a college student in a white uniform make sure the coffee was perfect and they had pastry there and any any faculty member could come now i had gone to such a thing as an undergrad as a graduate student at rochester they had a similar thing where prominent people came on in the in the bucknell uh it was for faculty only but you met other faculty there and you had a nice in the middle of the morning you know it was a break and my office was right across the quadrangle from uh this building the library building that was uh that was really a nice time but i didn't like the fact that there was no physics playing down there so um the physics faculty members were just teaching so every week i went over the hills and bucknell gave me a nice old 98 to drive over the hills to penn state every week just for that trip not to have during the week and um i tend to cloak in it was an hour's drive over the hills and then the little middle of winter the road went down to one cloud and lane which was a little nerve-wracking up over the mountains through the forests and as for a car the chairman of the apartment has said that he didn't like my car he would speak to a dealer and get me a deal on a much nicer car which he did to keep up the uh style he expected me to have anyway um oh actually you came out of the mountains here every week the cloakroom and finally the guys at penn state said hey why don't you just move over here i said that sounds like a good idea so i said well make me an offer they made me an offer and i said that's less than i'm earning a buck now okay all right all right we'll pay you whatever you paid it but no so we moved over to penn state now bucknell was so small the town was so small that was uh well to just give you an idea the water came from a stream up in the mountains and the biggest excitement in the town came while i was there was that the townspeople all got an uproar because somebody mentioned that they're probably beaver up there that peed and pooped in the city of water before it came down to the mountains well they wanted the beaver to be trapped out and other people said no no that was a big thing and the male students some of the male students would go down the susquehanna river in the spring there were huge ice flows in the river and um they stopped that one one of their canoes dumped them over just just before it got to a sharp point of a bridge of a foundation for a bridge that turned their canoe inside out anyway it was a sort of adventurous place hey hi yeah was at penn state and eventually we had a crazy dean who demanded to be on all grants and contracts so uh anybody that left could leave and about half the department left including me and i was sort of recruited by michigan state i was offered a better job and a third increase in pay and i said i could do whatever research i wanted i was doing a high energy interpretation of nuclear research at penn state because there was a nuclear group there mainly there was no high energy group there well i stayed in that field actually even michigan
